record.description:
Joe Tominaga was born in Coyote, California, a small town just south of San Jose. He grew up the fourth child of Japanese immigrant farmers. He moved to Los Angeles to work as a gardener while living with his sister and brother-in-law and eventually moved back to Berkeley to become a gardener until he was incarcerated during World War II. Tominaga discusses his experiences growing up as a Japanese American before WWII, in the incarceration camps, and after WWII.
While at Fresno Assembly Center, he volunteered to work as a sugar beet farmer so he and his family would be transferred to Topaz . While at Topaz, he worked in the Carrier Service, then as a truck driver, and finally as a foreman at Chicago Wirecraft Defense Plant until he was finally drafted into the Army. However, the war ended soon thereafter and thus he did not have to serve. He spent several years after the war in Chicago where he encountered racial prejudice in the workplace and decided to move back to Berkeley where he continued his work as a gardener to support his wife and son.
record.transcript:
REDMAN: Today is November 26th, 2012. My name is Sam Redman, and I'm sitting down today in Albany, California with Joe Tominaga. Joe, to begin, I wonder if you would do me the honor of stating and then spelling out your name for me.
TOMINAGA: Okay. The last name?
REDMAN: Yeah. So Joe is the first name spelled like 'Joe.'
TOMINAGA: Right. J-O-E.
REDMAN: And the last name?
TOMINAGA: Tominaga.
REDMAN: And could you spell that?
TOMINAGA: T-O-M-I-N-A-G-A.
REDMAN: And Joe, when were you born? What was your date of birth?
TOMINAGA: August 13th, 1917.
REDMAN: And whereabouts were you born?
TOMINAGA: I was born what they call the outskirt of San Jose. They call it Coyote.
REDMAN: Coyote, California.
TOMINAGA: Something like that anyway. That's what's on my birth certificate.
REDMAN: And it's a small place outside San Jose?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, you know, it's just a little area, outskirts of San Jose, southern part of San Jose.
REDMAN: Tell me about your parents. Who were your parents? Tell me about both your mother and your father.
TOMINAGA: My father came to Hawaii, Maui, in 1886, I think it was. Something like that. Anyway, near that.
REDMAN: And how about your mother?
TOMINAGA: My mother came later on when my father came to California.
REDMAN: And do you know how they met?
TOMINAGA: They were neighbors in Japan.
REDMAN: Did their families know each other in Japan?
TOMINAGA: Well, she was much younger, ten years younger, so, you know, when you're ten years younger, when you're growing up, big difference.
REDMAN: So maybe they didn't notice each other very much --
TOMINAGA: No, no. That's right.
REDMAN: But then when they were both in California they met again.
TOMINAGA: Right, yeah. My mother came over on a boat and came in over to the island over there.
REDMAN: What was your father doing -- oh, do you mean Angel Island?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, yeah.
REDMAN: Did she talk about that experience of going to Angel Island and arriving there -- ?
TOMINAGA: Well, not too much. She didn't say too much about that part.
REDMAN: How about your father? What type of thing did he do for work?
TOMINAGA: He worked in the sugar cane in Hawaii first. And he didn't like the way the boss ride the horse and crack the whip at him. And so he said he heard that "California, you'd like it, there is lot of work over there." So he took the boat and came to California.
REDMAN: And do you remember about what year that was?
TOMINAGA: No, I really don't. I didn't follow that.
REDMAN: I understand, and maybe this is something your parents may have talked about later, that just after you were born, not many years after you were born, the US passes some new laws that are known as the Asian Exclusion Acts that limit immigration.
TOMINAGA: Right.
REDMAN: But your parents, then, from the sounds of it, made it in to the United States --
TOMINAGA: Yeah, before that.
REDMAN: -- before that window. Did they talk at all about how those immigration acts -- ?
TOMINAGA: Well, no, my father was not what you call, or my mother was not what you call, really educated people in Japan.
REDMAN: How far along had they gotten along in school?
TOMINAGA: Well, my father, I think, got on into high school, but I don't think he graduated. That part I don't really know.
REDMAN: We were talking about your father and how far he had gotten in school.
TOMINAGA: Yes. I never did really ask him at all, so I wouldn't know.
REDMAN: But you'd guess it was about --
TOMINAGA: He said he just got into high school, I think, but then he was a fourth son, you know. In Japan, when you're way down, the fourth son, you're nobody.
REDMAN: There are fewer opportunities for the lower sons in terms of taking over an apprenticeship or a farm or something like that. So it's understandable that he would want to emigrate to the United States.
TOMINAGA: Yeah, right.
REDMAN: For an opportunity here.
TOMINAGA: Right. Right.
REDMAN: My impression is that that would have been a common story for a lot of Japanese young men like your father, and he probably would have found a community of Japanese Americans here in San Jose by that time. But it would have been a pretty small community still when you were born. Can you talk a little bit about what the community was like that you were born into and a little bit about that? I know it's early for you to remember, but maybe what your parents had told you about how you came up.
TOMINAGA: No, I was a -- see, I was a second son, the fourth one in the family. I had two older sisters and one older brother. Then I came in.
REDMAN: So you had a fairly big family then by the time --
TOMINAGA: Eight. Eight of us.
REDMAN: Eight of us. So then did your father keep farming and working farm labor here?
TOMINAGA: Yeah. I really don't know, but he was growing tomatoes in San Jose. Japanese could not own land, so all he did was sharecrop with a farmer. And that's how he got along. Was small, not very big. Horse and wagon days.
REDMAN: So this is some pretty old-fashioned farming.
TOMINAGA: Oh yeah, yeah.
REDMAN: Did you get the impression that it was hard physical labor for most guys?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. And my mother was a good worker.
REDMAN: So she also worked?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. She out in the field. In fact, I think she worked harder than my father did. [laughs] My father was an easygoing guy.
REDMAN: My next question was going to be a little bit about their personalities, who they were like as people. So you said your father was easygoing.
TOMINAGA: Yeah.
REDMAN: Your mother was a hard worker.
TOMINAGA: Right.
REDMAN: What else about them do you remember personality-wise?
TOMINAGA: Well, my mother would hate to lose to anybody, you know, when she was out in the field working and all. And I took after her and my one sister took after her. Two of us took after her. We were hard losers.
REDMAN: And how about your father?
TOMINAGA: Oh, no. Father just went along. Like I said, he was easygoing man.
REDMAN: So a number of people have told me there's a mixed portrait of what the Great Depression does in California.
TOMINAGA: Yeah.
REDMAN: So you would have been about twelve or thirteen. You would have been a young man when the stock market crashes.
TOMINAGA: Oh, yes. I remember that.
REDMAN: You remember that?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, in the thirties.
REDMAN: Before that, a lot of people have told me that times were already pretty tough, before the ledge falls off. How about in your experience? Did the coming of the Great Depression hit your family really hard, or was it already tough times?
TOMINAGA: Didn't have too much money, but I remember my dad going to the bank and the bank had no money, you know. It was Bank of Italy, they used to call it. Then I remember he went to the bank, and he couldn't get any money and didn't have too good of a job either. So how we got along with seven, eight kids, I don't know, but we got along.
REDMAN: Tell me a little bit about everyday life in that era. When you were a young man, you would wake up in the morning, and what was your day like and what was your family's day like? What were your duties around the house? Tell me a little bit about that.
TOMINAGA: Well, I had two older sisters and a brother, so naturally, when I was growing up, they did most of the work, and I was just one of the help along the side. And I just do whatever they told me to do. Say jump, I said, "How high?" [laughs]
REDMAN: How about school? What do you remember about school in the early days?
TOMINAGA: Well, I was fortunate enough to go through up to a junior in high school. But the time was still bad. And my mother did not believe in education. That's the bad part of it, see, and I still regret that today, and I hold it against her. I wanted to get educated. So anyway, my schooling went up to junior and then strawberry season, you know, there weren't too much money yet in '30, '34. So she made me quit school. My brother, older brother, he didn't like school anyway. He quit earlier, see, in sophomore year, I think. So anyway, I regret that I did not get education. That's why I made sure my son went right to the very top.
REDMAN: So that was something that left a major impression on you, it seems like.
TOMINAGA: Right.
REDMAN: I want to know a little bit about that place that you worked, that farm you must have worked at right after leaving high school. You said you worked in strawberry fields?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, before that. And I got tired of the farm. And my cousin, dad's cousin, lived in Los Angeles. He was a hard worker, you know, and he didn't have that much education either, but he visited us out there in Elk Grove, California. And after he came, he said, "Hey, you got lot of people here. Why don't you let me have one of your boys come over help me?" So anyway, right after that, I said, "Hey, good idea!" My brother was kind of against it, too, me leaving, but I packed up my suitcase, only one little suitcase, and I rode the train from Elk Grove to Los Angeles.
REDMAN: And this was in the mid-1930's?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, right in '34.
REDMAN: Tell me about what you see in LA when you arrive. How is Los Angeles different?
TOMINAGA: Oh, it was a big city, you know. Sacramento was the biggest city around, you know, that I been to, and I kind of felt like I was lost. But my cousin, he had three children too. He had a daughter little bit older than me, and a son, and another daughter. And they kind of helped me along. I went over there sort of like I was one of the family also.
But I didn't live with them. They had a boarding house across the street, my cousin did. And so that's where I stayed. I went over there, and I didn't know what kind of work I was going to do. So I decided, "Oh, maybe I'll try the market, wholesale market." I went in the wholesale market, and they put me right away in the back washing carrots and beets. And that wasn't for me, you know. I didn't like it, and they heard about it at the boarding house.
This one man, he was doing the gardening business and he says, "How about if you want to work for me?" So I said, "Oh, yeah. I will." I said, "What you got to do?" I didn't know what gardening was even, you know, out in the country. I went over there, and he said, "Well, this is it." I never pushed a lawnmower in my life. We didn't have a lawn. So I pushed that lawnmower, you know. And he said, "My gosh, I never saw anybody go so fast and do things and learn so fast!" So I worked for -- I think it was less than three dollars a day, if I'm not mistaken.
REDMAN: Tell me about -- this is an era in the 1930's when you arrived in Los Angeles, there was still a lot of racism --
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah, yeah.
Redman -- both culturally, but then also laws against hiring certain people. What was your experience? It seems like there were only a few types of jobs that young Japanese men could get in those days.
TOMINAGA: Yeah. Well, I don't know because most of them worked in the market. They worked in the market or they worked in a laundry. Only two place. And then the others worked as a gardener, a gardener's helper.
REDMAN: Let's step back for a moment and I want to hear about what languages were spoken in the home and how much Japanese you were learning and how much English you were learning and how you were learning Japanese and how you were learning English. Were you learning English in school? Were you speaking Japanese at home? How did that work?
TOMINAGA: Well, we had to learn to talk English, naturally, at school. But my mother would get after us and said, when we come home, she said, "Speak Japanese!" Well, we didn't know too good a Japanese. So my mother made us speak Japanese. She taught us a lot of etiquette about Japan around the table. You know, in the country, you have one table and you got benches you sit on. Eight of us sit around the table after dinner or after eating. My mother would sit there and tell us different stories. And lecture us, too.
REDMAN: So dinnertime, eating dinner as a family was very important.
TOMINAGA: Right. Always, always as a family. My father was the number one. Get served first. He gets to take whatever's out there. We had only so much to eat. He was a big eater, too. Sometimes, I said, "My gosh, he's eating everything and not leaving us anything!" [laughs]
REDMAN: Now, tell me a little bit more about Los Angeles. So you were in Los Angeles. You're working as a young man in the thirties. Now I understand again in 1937, the market takes another tumble and the economy again slips.
TOMINAGA: Well, that's when I was back here in Berkeley, though. See, 1935, in the end of 1935, during the summer, I visit Berkeley and my brother-in-law -- my older sister was living in Berkeley then. He said, "If you're going to do gardening" -- you know, he heard I was gardening, see, he was a gardener here, too -- he said, "Why work in such a hot place like Los Angeles? Why don't you come over here and live with us and work in Berkeley? You'll be closer to the family, too." So I said, "Oh, good idea!" So I went back, and I went over there and told my boss and my cousin. Oh, they didn't like it when I said, "You know, I made up my mind, and I'm going to go to Berkeley to work." "Why go to Berkeley?" I said, "Because I got my sister there." I travelled. I don't know how I did it on the train, young as I was. But I got there. I got here, anyway. [laughs]
REDMAN: What was Berkeley like when you first arrived? What impression did that give you?
TOMINAGA: Oh, Berkeley was a nice place. It was where your car could not stay out in the street, you know. Everybody had to put the car inside the driveway or the garage. They weren't prejudice at all, Berkeley, compared to other cities.
REDMAN: So in your experience, you felt like there was more prejudice in Los Angeles than in Berkeley.
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah. Right.
REDMAN: Do you remember whereabouts you lived in Berkeley when you first arrived?
TOMINAGA: I lived on Julia Street, right off of Ashby Avenue.
REDMAN: So tell me about the type of work you would do as a gardener in Berkeley in the 1930's. Who were your clients?
TOMINAGA: Well, first I worked for my brother-in-law's friend. They wanted a helper, you know. So there was one person name -- you know, who was very good at Japanese Garden and he had a son that was going to Cal and a daughter going to Cal. Oh, he taught me a lot about gardening. And I was only eighteen, nineteen years old.
And then I said, "Well, why work for somebody when I could work myself?" My brother had an old car, Model-A '31. He was going to sell me that because he's going to buy a little pickup. And so I bought that off him for, I don't know, $50 or so' $50 was a lot of money then, right. So I bought that, and I bought an old lawnmower. I didn't want anything new. Old, everything old. That way, I look like I'm an experienced guy. [laughs] So I bought it and started a few days of my own. Those days, you know, gardeners are working, if they had a route and they want to move to some other place, would sell their route to me. There was one fellow. He was going to move out to Castro Valley from Berkeley and work in a nursery. So I bought out his route. And that's how I got started. Very small, you know. Five dollars a day is all we made.
REDMAN: So you went from about $3 a day in LA to making about $5.
TOMINAGA: Right, five dollars on my own.
REDMAN: Did you have a different feeling about that work? Did you feel any different about that work when you started owning your own business and having your own clients?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. It was nothing like having somebody telling you what to do, how to do it, criticize what you do. I was on my own. The harder I worked, the more I made, see. I didn't just settle for $5 a day. Later on, I did a little more extra, little faster work, and take on a little more jobs. I had $7 a day. I made it.
REDMAN: In this time between '37 and '41, in the Pacific, there are more and more tensions. Japan and China go to war. And eventually, it's building up, to -- and then also the United States enters into something called the Lend-Lease Act with Europe, so we're building ships and munitions to send over to Great Britain. Was there a sense that World War Two was on the horizon? Or, as a young man working so hard in Berkeley, were you kind of oblivious to these major changes? Or were you aware?
TOMINAGA: Well, no. I didn't -- I wasn't too political then. But, you know, there was a fellow from Japan, Kurusu, came to talk with Roosevelt [Franklin D. Roosevelt] all the time. And they were talking, you know, but I just read a little bit in the paper. I remember that part of it.
REDMAN: [Phone rings] How about your parents? Did your parents have a political viewpoint or was your mother -- did she feel a certain way about Japan? [Phone rings] We can wait.
TOMINAGA: Anyway, no, my father and mother was not politically for Japan or anything. Naturally, they were from Japan, so they were, you know -- anybody from foreign country, like Italian, anybody that think of the place where they were born and raised. But my dad was never that -- what you call -- he had dual citizenship.
REDMAN: I see.
TOMINAGA: All of us boys had dual citizenship. Just before the war, something sensed him that he went to some place here in San Francisco, the Consulate General or something, he was told to go, and took our citizenship out of Japan before the war started even.
REDMAN: So he had a sense that the implications for you might be worse if you were dual citizens.
TOMINAGA: Right. Because there was a man about four houses down. He was for Japan. He had one son, too. And in fact, he sent his son back to Japan before Pearl Harbor, so he knew something. My dad and him was talking, so I wouldn't know what they talked about.
REDMAN: But your sense is that the Japanese community that was here knew that something was --
TOMINAGA: Well --
REDMAN: -- something was happening.
TOMINAGA: Some of them knew that who was political. They would follow more than --
REDMAN: But you as a young man, you weren't paying attention.
TOMINAGA: No, I wasn't. Didn't think nothing about Japan. In fact, I couldn't speak too good of Japanese either.
REDMAN: Did you have any feeling about Herbert Hoover as President?
TOMINAGA: Well, I kind of remember name only. Name only.
REDMAN: Then how about FDR? Franklin Roosevelt?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, well, he was -- he brought NRA [National Recovery Act], helped the country come back on their feet and all.
REDMAN: So there's a series of programs, the New Deal, including the NRA, which you mentioned, the National Recovery Act. There's also the CCC, Civilian Conservation Corps. Works Progress Administration.
TOMINAGA: Right. But they didn't help us Japanese. They only helped Caucasians.
REDMAN: Poor whites.
TOMINAGA: Yeah.
REDMAN: So you'd mentioned you feel like FDR helped the country with those things. But was that hard for you to watch? That these poor whites were getting relief but non-whites were excluded from those programs predominantly?
TOMINAGA: Well, in a way, it kind of hurt me. I was old enough to get hurt a little bit. But I said, "No, this is not fair. This is prejudice."
REDMAN: How about other examples of prejudice or racism?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. I was called a Jap all my life, kicked around, spit at, and everything else, at school and all that.
REDMAN: One of the things -- this is not a very fun thing to talk about -- one of the things I wanted to hear a little bit from you is some of the racial terms and the language and some of the pejorative, negative terms and slang that people would use. You'd mentioned Japs. Do you remember any other phrases? I've heard also a few others
TOMINAGA: Oh, "nips," "nips," "nips." They call us nips, Japs. What else? There was some other word they used to use against us. Slant-eye. They had everything you could think of.
REDMAN: So that was common around that time.
TOMINAGA: Right. Hell, I just took it as, what do you call it? "That's what they want call me, that's fine. They're satisfied with it, that's fine." I didn't argue about it. But if they hit me, I really give it back to them twice as much. I was a fighter.
REDMAN: So, the sense that I'm getting from you is that a young Japanese man just going about his day, maybe working, you might hear comments like the things that we said. And it might even be worse. You might get into a physical altercation with someone. Was there a sense -- did young men talk about these sort of experiences of prejudice and racism? Would you talk to other young Japanese guys afterwards?
TOMINAGA: No. My group never -- we never talked that much about prejudice. If I go to some meeting or church or something, once in a while and bring something up. But I let them talk the way they want. I didn't join them.
REDMAN: So you were aware that those things were going on.
TOMINAGA: Oh, they were going on. Yeah. Certain groups; they had groups. But I never did join any of those kind of group.
REDMAN: Let's talk a little bit about religion. Did your parents grow up religious at all?
TOMINAGA: Yes. My father did. He had a book. He wasn't too good, but he used to recite that Buddhist -- what's it called -- sing-along. I don't know. Then he'd hit a bell and that used to make me laugh. [laughs] He was a Buddhist, right. I did not -- my mother -- we never did go to a Buddhist Church. The only thing I heard Buddhism is when my dad [sang] at home or we go to a funeral. A Japanese funeral. And they'd all sing-along there for one hour. And I don't know what they're saying.
REDMAN: How about later on in Berkeley? Were you attending any church services?
TOMINAGA: Well, no. In Los Angeles is where I became a Christian.
REDMAN: Is that right?
TOMINAGA: I went to a Buddhist Church way downtown from -- I lived in the part of Los Angeles, the middle part of Los Angeles. Uptown District, they called it. And I had to ride the streetcar to go to J-Town. We called it J-Town. I went to the Buddhist Church over there because my mother said, "If you can, go to church." My mother at least told me that. So I went over there, and they talk all Japanese and sing a song and I says, "I don't understand." I came home and I told my cousin, a girl, you know, cousin, "Oh, gee, I went to Buddhist Church but I didn't understand anything." She said, "Why don't you come to our church?" And I said, "Where?" She said, "Right around the corner here! Episcopal Church." That's where they were going. The father was real Buddhist and all, but the children were all Episcopalian. And so I went there. Oh, I understood! And I really loved it. It was a short time.
First time I learned Christianity. I never had a chance before. I used to go to church up there in Sacramento area [during] Christmas time. In school, we had plays, you know, Christian plays all the time. Well, Christianity, right? That's the only Christianity I had in me. But I loved it so much. And then just before I left Los Angeles for Berkeley, the minister asked me, "Joe, you ever think about getting baptized?" I said, "What's that?" Then he explained to me what baptism was. He had me over to his home three, four times. And one thing he told me, "Joe, do not take religion too seriously." He said, "Some people do." I said, "Oh, yeah. No, I won't." So I became a Christian, Episcopalian, over in Los Angeles before I came over here.
REDMAN: And you continued, then --
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. But when I came here, Episcopal Church, no Japanese here in Berkeley. Only San Francisco. But I didn't want to travel there. So I went to a Free Methodist Church. Right now, it's in Richmond. I had my place in Berkeley first. Derby Street in Berkeley. Free Methodist. Anyway, I went over there, and they were a very strict church, Free Methodist. They said, "No skating, no dancing," and all. And here I am, growing up young man, and I didn't like that. So my friend was saying, "Hey, why don't you come to Berkeley Methodist Church on Carleton Street in Berkeley?" So I went over there before the war. I was just a member over there. I used to help with the athletics and all that over at the Berkeley Methodist Church.
REDMAN: Talk a little bit about that. The athletic program, sports programs affiliated with the church.
TOMINAGA: Yes. We had -- well, Berkeley had a club. What they call a Nisei club. It was Christian and Buddhist. So it was really nice. After we went to our churches, after that we'd have basketball. We'd go to this one place there and we meet and then we play basketball and all that. Nisei Club.
REDMAN: It sounds like a nice community.
TOMINAGA: Right, right. Oh yeah. And the Buddhist and Christian boys all got together.
REDMAN: So that cultural tie of being Japanese, and especially being second generation Japanese, was in many respects more important than what religion may have been, whether you were Christian or Buddhist.
TOMINAGA: Right, right.
REDMAN: Do you have anything else to add about the Japanese community in Berkeley?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, well, see I was -- and after the war even -- I mean, no, during the war, I helped, like I said, the athletic program, and I was the manager and all that, Berkeley Nisei. I never played basketball because I couldn't run up and down. I was smoking too much. But I love football. We had a good football team. In fact, Berkeley had a Little League, Japanese League that we played at the Rose Bowl in 19 -- what was it? Just a year before I was coming over to Berkeley, they played in the Rose Bowl halftime.
REDMAN: That must have been a heck of an experience.
TOMINAGA: Oh, they played nice. They played against a Caucasian team Berkeley had. The Japanese team and then there was a garage. What was it, I forget. I can't think what the name of the team was. Anyway, they played over there. I didn't get to play on that team. Even though if I was here, I was too old. I was one year too old to play on that team.
REDMAN: But it was still an aspect of the community that was very important. And then you got involved by helping manage some of these activities.
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah.
REDMAN: I see. Now let's talk about Pearl Harbor. December 7, 1941. What do you remember about that day?
TOMINAGA: I really remember that good. Always after church, we all get together, young people at Grove Park. There was a tennis court there, and that's where we used to always get together. Sometimes we have softball game over there against one another, but mostly tennis. And I had a girlfriend that used to play tennis a lot, so I used to, you know, sit around. I never played tennis. I tried, but I quit. But we were playing tennis, and all of a sudden somebody had the radio on then. Didn't have too much radio; we didn't have no small radio. It was in the car. He was sitting there. And he said, "Hey! Oh! Japan, what-do-you-call, Pearl Harbor!" I didn't know what it was all about, but that's when I first heard about it.
Actually, there were some Hawaiian boys and my brother that were all drafted one year before Pearl Harbor. And they were at Fort Ord. And you know the Japanese community over there, so a Hawaiian boy used to come over to Berkeley. They were all alerted to get back to their base.
REDMAN: That sounds like a very vivid memory for you, hearing that news.
TOMINAGA: Right, right. Really hit me, too! I couldn't believe it. I couldn't believe it until I read the paper, read the paper.
REDMAN: So it sounds like even though we talked about some of the political rumblings and your dad working your citizenship a little differently, that this was still a very big surprise to the Japanese-American community.
TOMINAGA: Right, right. Well it hit us. All, all of us! We couldn't believe it. We don't -- we just couldn't believe it.
My brother -- I got two brothers younger than me. They're twins, right under me. And they always used to like go to San Francisco. They heard about it too. So they went to San Francisco just to see how everybody was acting. And boy, FBI went in there and closed a lot of things right up, right away.
REDMAN: One of the things I wanted to ask about is that the FBI comes in and, I believe numbers in the thousands, there are several thousand people, including community leaders, ministers, things of that nature, that the FBI removes. It's still another few months until the order is posted for Japanese to be relocated. So there's a few months in there where there must have been some confusion, I imagine, over what was going on. The FBI is removing certain people. But what were you thinking about how this was going to affect you and your life?
TOMINAGA: No, it wouldn't affect me. I just made up my mind. I didn't care what my brother or whatever. I made up my mind -- I was still single -- I just go along what they tell me to do. They tell me to jump, I said, "How high?" I says, "Why fight it? You can't fight the government. Why fight it?" Go along, and then that way you get treated. I did that all the way through the war. That's why I got around. I got through the war very good.
REDMAN: So your mental attitude towards it was to try to just get through it and not cause any waves, so to speak.
TOMINAGA: Right, right. My dad, he didn't believe in that either. My mother, either. They didn't believe in any riot or anything. But my father always told us, "If they ever hit you, let them hit you first. But don't hit them first." But then, he says, "Go in and give them everything you got." That's the way my dad always was.
REDMAN: A number of people have told me that there's debate about the differences between the generations and their attitudes towards internment and what happened. And some people have argued that the older generation were taught some of those lessons of going along with the government, being subservient to leaders and elders, and that the younger generation of Americans may not have been quite the same. Can you talk about the cultural difference?
TOMINAGA: Well, as far as I'm concerned, my parents didn't say anything. They just kept quiet, and they said, "You can't have any this and that" and all. So my dad had anything Japanese, he'd burn it. We had records, all kinds of music. My brother used to like Japanese music, and we used to buy records that come from Japan. In fact, my dad took all those and burned them up, too. I don't know what for. He said, "Oh, that's Japanese!" So he says anything like that, Japanese, he says --
REDMAN: "Got to get rid of it."
TOMINAGA: "Get rid of it so we don't have any problem."
REDMAN: What was the role of the media, do you think, in shaping how people in California thought of the Japanese? I understand that there were some pretty nasty newspaper articles.
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah, yeah. I can name a few names, too, that was against us. Governor, number one. Mayor of San Francisco, number two. He was Italian on top of that. That's why the Italian people didn't get treated bad. That's the way I look at it. Okay. There was another man up north, what you call those? He had something to do with the government, and he was really against me. Found out after the war, I worked for this person and say "Name, Name. Colon." And his father was really against us. I worked for him. And we never talked about that at all. But I didn't know that! If I had known that, I wouldn't have worked for him. [laughs]
REDMAN: I understand that a lot of the fears that people had or the thoughts that were stated as one of the reasons why the Japanese needed to be removed from the coasts was a fear of espionage, a fear of spies. Did you hear a lot of that sort of talk?
TOMINAGA: Oh, I heard lot of talk. I heard lot of talk. I kind of belong to what they call a JACL, Japanese American Citizen League. They were very, what's it called, ahead, very ahead of that thing. I never cared for [Mike M.] Masaoka. Lot of people were with him, but he's against me. He didn't have to go talk for me. I talk myself, individual. He volunteered all this and that, and talking for us. Nobody told him he could talk for me. That's why I never did like him, and everybody couldn't understand why I don't like him. And I tell him the reason, Japanese. My friend really against me when I talk bad about him.
REDMAN: You have different perspectives on, in particular, organizations like the JACL. I wonder if I could ask about another group. I understand that some of the people associated with the Berkeley Methodist Church, and in particular some university folks -- the head of International House was a man named Alan Blaisdell, and he was trying to help young Japanese people at that time, including some college students. He would help them transfer. So it seems like there were some whites who were sympathetic to the Japanese situation. But, on the other hand, there was a lot of racism. So what was your experience?
TOMINAGA: With the students that come to BMU, they come from Fresno, everywhere, Christian. Every Sunday, we used to get together. I belonged to that group. They all thought -- I used to go around with so many of those university people, they thought I was still going to Cal, too. So one girl asked me, "Joe, when are you going to graduate?" You know, she was a freshman come in. I said, "I'm not going to school. I'm at the library with a bunch all the time and all, with a bunch of group."
REDMAN: So was your sense that the University was maybe doing something to help some of the students?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah, yeah. University was really trying to help. Later, after the war, too. Sproul, he really helped us. And his daughter, I forget the name. My sister worked for him, in fact. They really helped us.
REDMAN: In Spring of 1942, notices are being posted around Japantown, around Berkeley --
TOMINAGA: All over, right.
REDMAN: -- about a curfew, exclusion zones, having to register, and, eventually, removal. Can you talk about that experience? Hearing that you would have to have these special regulations before going to the camp, what was your reaction to that?
TOMINAGA: Well, first, anyway, we had a block manager that was head of every block that tells everybody what to do. [phone rings]
REDMAN: We can wait, that's fine.
TOMINAGA: Next door neighbor in Berkeley, I was his assistant going around telling everybody what to do and all.
REDMAN: So was this a Japanese man?
TOMINAGA: No. He's Caucasian.
REDMAN: Caucasian man, okay, who was a block manager. Okay. And you were his assistant? I see, okay.
TOMINAGA: [Phone ringing] I don't think it's my son. He knows that I have the interview.
REDMAN: All right, so go ahead. [Phone rings]
TOMINAGA: Four, four times. He'll ring four times. [Phone rings] I don't think it's my sister. I got a ninety-eight year old sister in a rest home.
REDMAN: That's just fine. All right, so you were the assistant to the block warden -- the block manager.
TOMINAGA: Right. And some people on the block couldn't understand why a Jap is over there telling them what to do. They told next door neighbor, I heard it around the way, that a certain person was talking. We didn't have any blacks in the neighborhood, mostly all Caucasian. We had only two blacks in my street over there.
REDMAN: So what were some of the duties early on? There were blackouts. So the block warden would have to see that everybody was --
TOMINAGA: Right, right, yeah. We got to make sure that they wanted black paper, roofing paper and all. If we had some, we give it or get it from somebody and help them put it up there even. I did that. There was some older people that couldn't do that, you know? The blackout.
REDMAN: But you still experience racism in doing this.
TOMINAGA: Right, right, right. I hear it from the other people. "What's a Jap doing over here?" They're fighting us.
REDMAN: Was that hard for you?
TOMINAGA: Oh, in a way, it was kind of hard on me. But I just let that go through.
REDMAN: How about the curfew?
TOMINAGA: Yeah. Curfew, we had to watch everything all the time. We couldn't go.
REDMAN: So do you remember, did that affect you in any particular --
TOMINAGA: Well, it affected us a lot of ways. We couldn't go anywhere; we had to stay home. So anyway, they said we had to go. They're going to round the first generation, Issei, up. So I was preparing for that. My brother was, like I said, in the Army one year before Pearl Harbor. So I was number one man of the home. I was more or less number one anyway, but, you know, Japanese style, number one is number one.
REDMAN: I'm going to pause this for one moment. [brief pause in tape] So your brother is in the Army. Could you just summarize briefly for me, before we get into your experience during the war, what was his experience during the war?
TOMINAGA: Well, I don't know, not too much anyway. They said they were going to draft. He was in the first call. So he got called. He was one of the first Japanese in Berkeley that got called. When he got called, he was all -- he didn't mind. He said, "Well, if I got to go, I got to go." And he said, "Joe, you got to take over" I said, "Yeah, I know I got to take over the whole family." So I was number one man.
Anyway, when he got inducted, they gave him -- the Boy Scout Troop, they heard about it, and they thought it was an honor for him to go in. They came all the way to Tyler Street -- that's one block other side of Ashby -- and all the way to Oregon Street is a Buddhist church there. They marched him all the way in a parade. They paraded, you know, with a drum and bugle corps, they paraded. And he had an honor to go in, and he thought it was really great.
REDMAN: But you ultimately decide not to join.
TOMINAGA: Who?
REDMAN: You.
TOMINAGA: No, not that. I couldn't join. They didn't call me, you know?
REDMAN: After Pearl Harbor, the rules were different.
TOMINAGA: No, no. See, this is it. In camp, you know, they had -- they were going to draft, right? Hawaii wanted to make a certain group. And so, they had the United States, too. They were going to draft. Lieutenant Tracy came in to Topaz. I was in Topaz. I'm in the Carrier Service. In fact, I picked him up and brought him into camp. My brothers, twin brothers, were the first two to sign up with him, Tracy. And then a few other guys in the block, they encouraged those guys to volunteer, too. So they volunteered right away in Topaz.
REDMAN: So they didn't end up joining the 442nd, did they?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, yeah. That was -- after they go in, that was the 442 that they were going to join. Twin. One of them, he had bad eye one side. And he wanted to go in so bad with his brother, that he cheated and looked from the other side eye. And they went to Salt Lake and they caught up with him and they threw him out. I thought he was going to commit suicide. I was really worried about him, you know, because he couldn't go.
REDMAN: But that really, really affected -- I've heard that about some of the men who were declared 4-F, who were declared ineligible. That was a very hard thing because some people thought or some people may have thought it had something to do with your masculinity, whether or not you were man enough. Obviously, with your brother, he couldn't control his eyesight.
TOMINAGA: Right, right.
REDMAN: But he took that hard emotionally.
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah, yeah. And so anyway, then right after that, the welding school opened. The government going to teach them free to learn to weld so they could help. He heard about that. He went to Salt Lake and he took welding. And from there on, that's what made the story. They couldn't get a job around here, so they said, "Oh, Chicago, you could go in." So they took the train and went to Chicago, him and a bunch of guys. So they got a job -- they went over there to get a welding job. No. No welding job. They wouldn't hire Japanese for it. Only place they could work was an ice company. They were pulling ice first, at, I think, thirty five cents an hour. But that was enough to make them live -- lived on the Southside. See, Southside in Chicago was divided. Cottage Grove. Black on one side and White and Jewish on this side. So Japanese were lucky enough to be on the Jewish side.
REDMAN: Do you recall registration, having to register yourself with the government?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. We had to register everything. Give us everything with the government. We had to have a card all the time. The FBI checked you out good. Any job, any time you go out of camp to work -- I volunteered at camp twice -- out.
REDMAN: I'm going to pause this. One second. [Brief pause in tape]
Let's cover that that you were just mentioning, that you left Berkeley for a short time. This was just before the start of the war?
TOMINAGA: No, after the war came on.
REDMAN: So the war comes on and you leave Berkeley.
TOMINAGA: Yeah. After the war come on, I was still single. Then they said all the people were going to get taken in. So I had to run the family with the other brothers, four other brothers. Somebody had to watch it. So I says, "I better get married." And I got married. I have to go to Stockton for a party, and I met my former-girlfriend, childhood girlfriend. We went over there and I says, "How's your boyfriend?" She said, "I'm just in-between now." I was just in-between my girlfriend and we happen to get together. I could still travel yet, even with the war. So I went to Stockton, you know, after work, and go to the movie and then come home. Then after that, they put a curfew on us too.
REDMAN: There must have been a lot of people in Stockton at that point working in agriculture.
TOMINAGA: Yeah, there were, right.
REDMAN: But the Japanese community in particular, there were a number of Japanese in Stockton. One more question. May I ask a question about the Bay Area?
TOMINAGA: Yeah.
REDMAN: And in particular, there are these new bridges that have just gone up, the Bay Bridge and the Golden Gate Bridge.
TOMINAGA: I saw both of them. I went through both of them right in the beginning.
REDMAN: Did you have to wear an identification or a marker that said you were Japanese when you were crossing the bridges?
TOMINAGA: No, never, never. If we're driving across the Carquinez, even the Carquinez Bridge, they stop and ask us for our driver's license. When they see our driver's license, then we go through. But that's after the war started.
REDMAN: You would just have to show your driver's license.
TOMINAGA: Right. That's all. We had no other identification. And then later on, they said, "You got to carry your birth certificate around." I didn't have any, so I drove all the way to San Jose to pick up my birth certificate.
REDMAN: Eventually you were given an order to remove to go to an Assembly Center, I assume. Can you talk about that?
TOMINAGA: Well, after we went to Elk Grove, it was understood that my brother-in-law's brother -- you know, he was a big time farmer; he was one of the biggest Japanese farmers in that area, growing sugar beets -- he said, "Well, after I plant my sugar beets" -- he had the land all ready. We planted it, we thinned it, and all that. And I went out there. First time I worked sugar beets. It was tough job, thinning. So I learned how to handle sugar beets, plant and thin and everything else. And after that, we were going to load up the truck, and take my parents, too, and take one truck with -- we had three trucks to go with. He had one truck to put our furniture and one truck to take the equipment we had.
But then after that, they said no more travelling for us. So we couldn't travel anymore. We were stopped right there. All that was gone. We were going to Texas. The Texas Governor says, "No Japs!" He said right out in the paper, so we didn't go. I thought we were going to go to farm. And if he didn't say that, we probably would have been in Texas during the war. But we couldn't go. So we had to wait. Then they said that area -- "You go to either Manzanar or Fresno Assembly Center." They said, Manzanar got all kind of scorpion and all. We didn't want to go there in the desert. So we said, "Well, we'll go to Fresno," So my brother-in-law and us all went on a train, and we went to Fresno Assembly Center. We were the last ones in there. Block J, way in the corner. Every time we went to any of these camps, it was always late. We went to Fresno Assembly Center.
REDMAN: I'm going to pause right there.
REDMAN: My name is Sam Redman. Today is November 26. I'm in Albany, California with Joe Tominaga, and this is our second tape together today. All right, Joe, when we left off, you had just arrived by train at the Fresno Assembly Center. Now, who exactly was with you when you arrived at Fresno? Your brother?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. My whole family. And my brother-in-law's family.
REDMAN: Did they have to make particular preparations before getting on the train?
TOMINAGA: Oh, no, no. We were understood that we had to -- since we moved to Elk Grove with our furniture and all, we had to take care of all that. We just had a certain amount of days to do that. So we went to buy ply board and everything. And they said the government will pick it up and put it in a warehouse for us. So we put everything in a ply board box. We made the box fast. And my dad was a good carpenter, too. So we all worked and nailed the box together and put all our furniture in there and Bekins came over there and took it to Sacramento, some warehouse. That's why we got to save all our furniture, too.
REDMAN: Your family ultimately did get their furniture back.
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. Yeah.
REDMAN: Tell me about Fresno Assembly Center. I'd like to hear first about the living conditions.
TOMINAGA: Well, you know, we had to live in these barracks which -- they wasn't the greatest thing. But everybody was divided into certain areas of barracks. One family in one area, my sister in another area, and my wife and I -- we were just married -- we were put into another area with another man and his daughter, a younger daughter. And my wife said, "I am not going to stay with them." So I went to the headquarters and I said, "No, we don't want to stay in there. Can you find another place? Or can we live with my sister?" My sister had a big area, so we went in there. My nephews were small yet, so we lived with them for a few months.
We lost my dad, July in '42, in Fresno Assembly Center. In fact, they didn't have a good hospital or doctors, so it was Fresno Assembly Center. Right across was the park over there. There was a hospital, county hospital, and that's where they put my dad. And we got to go see him there once a day. Only the family, one family a day. So my wife and I went over there to see my dad just the day before he passed on. One thing he told me, he said, "Joe, this is your country, you and the boys. Stand up for your country." That's what he told me before he died. I never thought he would ever tell me that. That's what he told me. So you can see that he never had any prejudice. He was one of the first generation I think ever talk like that.
REDMAN: So you think most of the first generation -- your father would have had a pretty unique -- that surprised you, that he felt that way about the United States.
TOMINAGA: Yeah, it really surprised me that he was that way because majority of the first-generation people I heard or talk or anything, that they were really against United States and talk bad. My dad never talked once -- I never heard him once say anything bad about United States.
REDMAN: It must have been hard as a young married couple to suddenly be thrust into this living condition where you're sharing close quarters with --
TOMINAGA: Well, yeah no, but we had curtain and all to divide it. So it wasn't too bad. You take it as you come and make the best of it.
REDMAN: My next question, then, was going to be about the mood, the general mood of the camp. You'd mentioned some of the differences between how the first generation and the second generation talked about the government during that experience. What else can you say about the mood in Fresno Assembly Center?
TOMINAGA: Well, food was food. And during the war, you know, they're limited too, right. The meat and all that. So, no, it wasn't too bad.
REDMAN: Oh, I meant the "mood," so how people felt.
TOMINAGA: Oh, how we felt about it? About what, now?
REDMAN: About being in the Assembly Center.
TOMINAGA: Well, no. We know what we had to do. Like some would people even hate to go. There was one friend of mine, San Leandro, you heard of the name, that really went against them. And I was against him, too. But, no. I said, "Go where they tell you to go. Jump when they say 'Jump'!" And that was my -- I told my wife the same thing. She was a quiet person. That's her over there, a very quiet person that did anything that I said to do.
REDMAN: Did you have a sense that you would eventually go to Topaz when you were in the Assembly Center? Or was there still confusion about what -- ?
TOMINAGA: No, no. I would go anywhere they tell me. Number one, from Fresno, we didn't want to go to Jerome, Arkansas. That's where the Fresno Assembly Center was going to go. So I went to the headquarters in the front office, and I said, "How can we make a transfer to Topaz, Utah with the San Francisco group all went there?" They said, "Well, only one way to do is volunteer out, and then you can pick any camp you want."
So I found out that in Montana, they wanted sugar beet people. So I was going to take my family and go to Montana. We was all set. Then my brother-in-law says, "Oh, I want to go with you guys." "Okay," I said, "Sure, you can come." And then his brother, who's a big-time farmer, he said, "Oh, can I come along, too?" I said, "Oh, yeah. You can come along as our group." And so we had quite a few people to fill the train up, went all the way to Montana with the shade down. We didn't know where we were going. And we had a soldier in front end and back end of the car all the way. So we went along. It took couple of days to get there. We got there, and the farmer picked us up, and he took us in the farm and he said, "Well, this is where you going to live."
And you know where it was? That's where he used to keep his hogs before. We said, "No way we're going to say there." I saw this motel-like cabin, leaving town, over there. And I said, "Hey, how about if we could go over there, and we'll rent that and stay there?" He said, "I'm not going to pay for it." I said, "I didn't ask you to pay for it." I said, "Can we stay there, and then you give us the truck to go back and forth to work?" It's about two miles from there to where his town was. "Oh, yeah, we can do that." So we went over there. We took one, two, three, four of those cabins. And that man was really glad, too, because he had no one to take them. He had about eight of them over there. And then this other Japanese bunch of group went with us, too, another group; they all came in there. They all came in there. So we had one place Japanese, all in there.
Funniest thing, when went to Malta, Montana; that's where we went. There was a Japanese family over there, had a little restaurant. [laughs] We were surprised to meet a Japanese family all the way in Malta, Montana. You know, way up there.
REDMAN: How long did that episode last when you're in Montana with this group?
TOMINAGA: Well, just the sugar beet season when we went to harvest the beets. And they had beets only big as California carrots. Oh, they were terrible. My brother-in-law -- we just laughed out there. We couldn't make any money with such small beets. California had beets real big, but over there it was just like carrots. We call it "California carrots."
REDMAN: So you were allowed to save some of this money?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. We didn't make much at all. We just made enough to eat, pay for the cabin, and a few dollars.
REDMAN: You had arranged to do this in order to get the transfer --
TOMINAGA: Yeah, to Topaz.
REDMAN: -- to Topaz. So that ended up working out?
TOMINAGA: Yeah.
REDMAN: Were the other people in your group hoping also to go to Topaz?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, my brother-in-law's brother wanted to go. His wife's family was in Colorado. Amache, Camp Amache. So they went there. And I don't know, the others, they went back to Fresno, some of the group.
REDMAN: Before I ask you a series of questions about what life was like for you in Topaz, I wonder if you could talk just a little bit about the differences between those camps that you've mentioned and the degree to which there was any communication between people that maybe had different experiences at the different camps. Or was it a pretty similar experience?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. All of it. Federal, you had bosses on top of you. You worked as a group. In Fresno Assembly Center, I had a good job. I went in there on the maintenance. I went late, so I had a hard time finding job. You know, when you go in there late, everybody took all the jobs already, right? So anyway, I went in the maintenance group, you know, clean up everything and do a little cement work and all that. I did a lot of cement work and all. The boss heard about that. He had all these other country boys at Fresno, they were just farmer boys that didn't know anything. So I got to be a foreman right away, soon as the boss saw what I could do, the Caucasian fellow. So I got along good. I did a lot of cement work, finishing, carpentry work. I could do all that.
I could drive truck. We used to go out to throw rubbish away to the dump. And on the way there, there's a little grocery store. We're really not supposed to stop, but the boss, very nice, he was very nice. I think he was a CO, Conscientious Objector, that's what a lot of those -- they worked at the camp, you know, COs. So they let us stop and buy cookies or whatever, the grocery store. The grocer, he was real happy, too. He made lot of money off of us. I would buy -- if I had in my pocket, I'd buy oh, five-dollars' worth. And my sister would give me so much for it, or neighbors who wanted cookies and all. They had money, so they give me --
REDMAN: But it was hard, if not impossible for them to get to the grocery store.
TOMINAGA: Right, right. They couldn't do nothing. We had a little canteen-like over there, but they only had certain things you could buy.
REDMAN: Let's talk for a moment about Topaz when you first arrived, what it looked like. And then you'd mentioned all of the different construction projects that you yourself are working on. I understand that the camp was built-up during the duration of the war. What are some of the things they built there? More barracks? A school?
TOMINAGA: Well, no. When we went in there, it was end of '42, end of '42, November 11 or 12. We finished the beets in Montana and then we went into Topaz. And naturally, we were last ones, so way back in Block 42. They had forty-two blocks. We were in the very corner block, right? That's where we was set. My mother -- because I lost my dad already -- my brother had one place, my sister and her family another one. And my wife and I, we went into a small apartment they had. They had all different sizes of place to live.
REDMAN: Were there any interactions with nearby Native Americans from Utah?
TOMINAGA: No. No, we never had any because none of them could come in.
REDMAN: So the only people that you really saw or interact with were the --
TOMINAGA: Were those GIs up there in the tower with their guns pointing at you.
REDMAN: How did that make you feel?
TOMINAGA: Well, that didn't make me feel -- that's why I told I would never go near a fence. My mother, she nearly got shot because she would hunt for arrowheads. There're a lot of arrowheads all over there. And you ought to see all the arrowheads, small -- in fact, I threw a bunch away -- all the little shells she used to pick up. She was very good. She had a lot of arrowheads, too. I didn't want any. But she went along the fence, and boy, they were going to -- somebody says they were going to take a shot at her. So they hurry up and pulled her away from the fence. They thought she was trying to go through the fence.
REDMAN: You'd mentioned that, for instance, when you're riding on the train to go to Montana, that there was an armed soldier on either side of the car. Did you have any interactions with those GIs, or was it mainly that they were a silent -- ?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, they were just silent. They'd just make sure that no trouble would come into us as well as trouble going out, if they would sneak out, you know. I think it was -- to me, it was more to protect us than to -- nobody stop at those stations, anybody who crazy could come in there and take a shot at us.
REDMAN: How about the weather? What do you remember about the weather?
TOMINAGA: Oh, it wasn't the best of weather. Raised in California all the way through, winter was really cold. In fact, we're loading on November 10; we had a bunch of beets that we had already cut and piled up. You know beets; you pile them up. It was out there, and it snowed like everything that night. Next morning, farmer came and said, "Hey, we're going to go there and pick up rest of the beets." Frozen beets were thrown on the truck. I can remember that. It was cold.
REDMAN: One of the things that I understand is that a lot of, especially Japanese in San Francisco, going from that climate to Topaz, would have been a real surprise. Let's talk about clothing for a minute. Was that a problem?
TOMINAGA: Well, see, we only had to go with a suitcase, so we couldn't take too much. We didn't know where we were going to go or anything. So I guess some people had took the winter clothes; some didn't. We got pea coats. Everybody got jackets. That's the only way you kept warm.
REDMAN: I understand that if you had to buy clothing, you might be able to buy it through a Sears catalog or another type of catalog. Could you talk just a little bit about that?
TOMINAGA: We didn't buy any clothes because we were out, we went out to work. Naturally, we went into regular store to buy. So we didn't do anything with a catalog.
REDMAN: At camp, there were certain things that you could buy?
TOMINAGA: Well, I didn't see any clothing in those canteens. It was just more or less odds and ends. Tablets, pencils, and stuff like that.
REDMAN: So if you did want, say, a new pair of pants or something, you --
TOMINAGA: Yeah, you had to go there and order through catalog. Or your friends give you -- you trade though a friend if you could.
REDMAN: It seems like the railroad and railroad coordination was a very important part --
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. It's the only way you travel, with the railroad.
REDMAN: The other thing that I was going to ask about that is that you'd mentioned that the shades were drawn on your trip up. It seems like that's a common experience for the Japanese when they're transported, is that they're put in these cars and then the cars are intentionally sort of blinded so you can't --
TOMINAGA: So no one could see us. I think that was more or less so the outsider couldn't see us and take a potshot at us.
REDMAN: I understand that everybody was assigned a letter, or assigned a number --
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. Yeah.
REDMAN: -- but that at the end of the number, you would have a letter based on your position in the family. Do you remember anything at all?
TOMINAGA: No, I don't remember a number. My wife, she was one of these persons that threw everything away. And she threw a lot of my things away. I had a few cards, too. A driver's license of different states. Every state I went to, I got driver's license, and I had my card, and I had a card that was really twenty-four hours. See, when I was in Topaz I was on the carrier service, so I had -- very few people had a twenty-four hour pass.
REDMAN: What does that mean, a carrier service?
TOMINAGA: Well, it's taxi-like. In camp. In camp and then I go to the train about six miles away from Topaz. Delta. And I was first a truck driver, went in as a truck driver. Because I went in late; there were no jobs. And it so happened that there was a man in the motor pool that I kind of knew a little bit. And that's the reason I got a job as one of the trucks they give me. They gave me an old junk truck to drive. They found out later that I used to truck from Sacramento to San Francisco before the war. I saw the Bay Bridge built right from the ground up when they were pouring the cement because we used to go with the ferry from Berkeley to San Francisco. I seen all that before I moved to Berkeley.
REDMAN: You were done with school at this age, but I'm going to ask you just a couple questions about schools in the camps to get a little more information about it. I wonder if you could talk a little bit about the experiences of what those young students may have been going through, if you know. And who were the teachers? And what types of things do you think that they were learning?
TOMINAGA: Well, I don't know. I didn't do much in schooling over there. I didn't have nothing to do with them. I had friends that went to University of Cal, and they couldn't get a job. They were teachers over there. That's it, about it.
REDMAN: So some of those Cal students who were Japanese would have found jobs in the schools maybe teaching some of the younger students.
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah, yeah. They had good teachers, I hear. As I understand. They were really strict, too.
REDMAN: Can you talk about music in the camps? Or singing or group singing? Was that an important part --
TOMINAGA: Well, no. Church had their singing. I never heard of any Japanese group singing. They have karaoke and all that now, but I never heard of any of that. My mother was an entertainer, actually. She knows all the old Japanese -- that's how she was brought up. That's why she didn't get educated in Japanese. I hate to tell you this but my mother did not know how to write Japanese, because she didn't go to school. She know how to speak, she know all the etiquettes and everything, but she did not know how to write Japanese. That's why she didn't believe in education. And that really hurts me right still today.
REDMAN: Let's talk just a moment about church in the camp. Describe what they experience was like and how it may have been different.
TOMINAGA: Well, church, they couldn't have too much. They just had a little, one little barracks they had for church. It didn't matter if you were Presbyterian, or Methodist, or Baptist, you all went to one church. And they had some good ministers there, too.
REDMAN: Well, they may not have had all the same resources that the churches in Berkeley or San Francisco may have had. There was still good ministers and good services, you felt.
TOMINAGA: Right.
REDMAN: I want to ask about the loyalty questionnaire, the questionnaire that includes the questions of, "Are you willing to serve in the Armed Forces?" and "Are you loyal to the United States?" And famously, individuals who answered "No" on both of those questions were referred to as "No-No Boys."
TOMINAGA: Right. And I was against all of them.
REDMAN: Tell me about the loyalty survey and what your reaction to that was. There are these boys that are joining the 442nd. There're the No-No Boys. So there're some differences in opinion and different reactions to that questionnaire. What was your reaction and your view on those other reactions?
TOMINAGA: Well, I said each one for their own. They could go that way, and we go our way. So I never had anything -- I didn't feel that -- we had only one member in the family, my brother-in-law's nephew. He was raised in Japan. He was born here, but he was raised in Japan. And his wife, she was raised in Japan too. She was one of the persons that used to go over there and do everything. They were the real No-Nos. They got taken into Topaz into this other camp. And they got taken over there. He was a nice quiet guy, but his wife was the one that got him in there.
REDMAN: The individuals that were No-No Boys, and they were then subsequently removed from Topaz and sent to special camp.
TOMINAGA: Most all of them were sent to Tule Lake.
REDMAN: Tule Lake. How about the legacy of the 442nd? Obviously, you pointed to a brother who joins earlier. But then, the number of Japanese in the armed services grows. I understand, after the camp experience, they start to send some of those boys over to Italy and over to Europe and other places. What do you think the legacy of the 442nd is and what does that mean for Japanese Americans now?
TOMINAGA: I don't know. My brother over there who was a minesweeper under me, he got a grave over here on Sunset. He got written on there, "I Served for My Loyalty." That's why my brother served. In fact, most all of them served to show their loyalty, that they are loyal to United States.
REDMAN: Were you proud of your brother?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. I was proud of my brother. There were one, two, three of them. Three, yeah, three of them went to Europe and one went to Japan. He could speak a little bit better Japanese. So he was a sort of interpreter, went to Japan, more or less, more after the war was over, naturally.
They wanted me to come in. They kept sending to me from Camp {Savvy?}, kept sending me letter all the time. Somebody in there knew me.
REDMAN: And knew you knew Japanese, or some Japanese.
TOMINAGA: Yeah, well I could speak enough, get along. I could speak enough. Sometimes, some people ask me, "When did you come to America?" [laughs] No, I know enough Japanese to get around. I go to Japan and they call me Gaijin. That means "foreigner," but still.
REDMAN: We talked a little for a moment about food. But I want to ask about eating in the mess halls. One of things that we talked about earlier in the interview was how important it was for a family to eat as a unit in Japanese culture. For the family to eat together. Now, I understand that when people go to camp, they're now eating in more like mess halls.
TOMINAGA: Yeah, right. All mess halls.
REDMAN: Do you think that that disrupted a little bit of the normal Japanese family?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah, the family get-together. Some family, they were very close. They make sure all of them sat around table. But my family, we just sat anywhere we wanted. Anywhere. [With] our friend[s].
REDMAN: So it seems like now, instead of sitting with the family, that the brothers would sit with the other young men, the sisters --
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah, yeah. They group girls with girls, or whatever.
REDMAN: In your mind, you think that that does change some of the social experience?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah, yeah. Family closeness disappeared there.
REDMAN: Do you think that that had an impact on the real little ones? The little kids?
TOMINAGA: Well, the little kids had to eat with the family always. They couldn't go.
REDMAN: But maybe for the teenagers or other young people, it was a bigger deal.
TOMINAGA: Yeah, they didn't want to eat with the family.
REDMAN: What do you remember about the food?
TOMINAGA: Oh, the food was all right unless they send us liver or tongue, ox tail. Those things, the Japanese never ate. And they didn't like lamb either too much.
REDMAN: And then how about bread rather than rice?
TOMINAGA: Oh, no. We had lot of rice all the time. Rice all the time. We had bread, too, lot of time, without rice. But most of the menu -- it was up to the cook to cook how we wanted it.
REDMAN: Now one of things that I've heard is that the camps were so large that they needed multiple cooks. And some of the cooks were better at cooking than others, so you'd want to get into certain mess halls.
TOMINAGA: Certain mess halls taste lot better than the other. See, I used to deliver coal, too, to all these mess halls. See, when I first driving the truck in camp, when I first went in there. My wife worked at a mess hall in our block. She did no other job, so naturally she was one of the servers.
REDMAN: Do you recall about how much she was paid to do that?
TOMINAGA: Well, they were only paid, how much was it? Fifteen -- no, was it $15; I think it was cheaper.
REDMAN: Fifteen dollars a month?
TOMINAGA: Month.
REDMAN: Whereas in Berkeley, you as a gardener were working and getting five dollars a day, so that's a big difference. Anything else about the mess halls?
TOMINAGA: No, that's all. Mess halls was just -- they go over there and hammer the pan is ready, so we go in, and that's it.
REDMAN: Were there ever any performances at dinner time? Like theater groups or anything like that? Or entertainment?
TOMINAGA: No, no. We had nothing. I didn't hear anything anywhere. We just all ate our meal and what we didn't eat took into our barrack. And certain time, we knew, since my wife working in there, what they're going to have. They're going to have liver, we didn't want liver. So my two brothers used to work commissary. And commissary, they can go over there and they got eggs and all that. They can go in there and take a few eggs out of the box before they deliver. That's stealing, but still, that's eggs on the side. So they bring that egg home and keep it over there. And we have a frying pan, a big pot-belly stove, and then they bring the rice in from the mess hall, and we make our own dinner if we didn't like to eat liver.
REDMAN: This is a question that's totally different. Going to the bathroom, I understand that most of these barracks would have had outhouses. But it was so cold that I understand a lot of people innovated bed pans.
TOMINAGA: Well, that I don't know. We never.
REDMAN: So you'd go to the outhouse?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, we'd go to the outhouse.
REDMAN: And do you remember that as being cold?
TOMINAGA: Oh, was kind of cold or cold air come up, but you get used to it. You just get used to all that.
REDMAN: How about bathing and personal hygiene at the camps?
TOMINAGA: Well, it was good, shower, you go in and sure, you're showering with the other people, but you mind your own business, that's it.
REDMAN: Can you talk about sports or sporting events?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. We had good sports. Basketball. Outdoor, we had baseball. We didn't have football over there because we didn't have the equipment. You get hurt. They had touch football, a few guys would play. But no, I was kind of old; I didn't join them. My younger brother used to play for basketball team. He was really one of the top players over there in the camp. There was All-Star team and he made the All Star team and all that. My youngest brother. He lives in Chicago today.
REDMAN: What do you think that meant for those young people to have those sports and sporting events, those things to do? But did it mean anything more symbolically in terms of having freedom or being an American and playing baseball? A lot's been made of that.
TOMINAGA: Yeah, I think to me that there was one way to enjoy themselves, too. And competitive, everybody wanted to be competitive all the time. That's it. We had some good players over there. In fact, there was one fellow named, from San Jose, Lefty Honda. He could have played Major League Baseball, he was so good.
REDMAN: Were there other particular activities for young people? What would young people do in camp for entertainment? And as a young married man, what would you do with your wife for entertainment?
TOMINAGA: No, we didn't. I didn't care to dance. They had dances and all. My brother was teaching dance, younger brother. But we didn't go for any of that. We just sat at the watchucallit. But we were out of camp. Actually, I didn't stay in camp too long. Six months in Fresno and then six months in Topaz. All the rest of the time I was out, always out working. I went, see, during the Spring. And from Topaz, I went out to sugar beets again in Malad City, Idaho.
REDMAN: So that was in 1943.
TOMINAGA: Forty-three.
REDMAN: So you're again working the sugar beets, this time in Idaho.
TOMINAGA: I was helping the war effort. See, that's what I went out for. Not to make money. For the war effort. And then I went out to Malad City, Idaho, I went out to Montana, you know.
REDMAN: So that lasts through '43. So you're working the fields in '43, but you don't come back to camp, then, after that.
TOMINAGA: Yeah, I came back to camp. I was corresponding with my brother who was in Chicago, and he said, "Joe, instead of working out on the farm, why don't you come to Chicago? There's all kinds of work that you could do." So naturally, right after that, I had all in my mind to go to Chicago. And the government gave me my train fare to go. So my wife and I, we packed our suitcase again and went to Chicago. When I went to Chicago, I said, "Where did I come? Dirty. Smoggy."
REDMAN: So this in 1944.
TOMINAGA: End of '43.
REDMAN: So at the end of '43, you and your wife go to Chicago. Tell me more about what you discover in Chicago.
TOMINAGA: At first, I didn't know where to go to work. My brother was working in one place where they had all kinds of jewelry, vases, and different things like that. So he said, "Hey, there's a job over here if you want to pack vases and things to ship out. Glassware." Delicate stuff. So I worked there one week. Only thing, we had lot of newspaper, and I'm a smoker. I couldn't stand that, without smoking for so long. So I said, "This is not a place for me to work." I told the boss, "I'm sorry, but I can't work here long." I told him the reason why. He said, "Oh, yeah, yeah. I know." So I left.
My brother, at the same time, he didn't have a job either. So he and I went one day up and down this one street to find a job. We saw a sign said "Help Wanted." And we went in there and this man told us, "Oh, no, but I got a friend that looking for a worker." So we went to this place, and that was the best thing we did. Went in there, and put us to work, tomorrow, come in. So we went in there, on Ashland Avenue in Chicago. And I went in there, and they have a defense plant. Small. Chicago Wire Craft. I went in there and he had a whole pile of things running on a press to cut certain length, got to be exact, every one of them.
REDMAN: Of wire?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, no. Aluminum. Aluminum. They was making radio guard. I signed on there and they went out to coffee, the boss and his assistant. I had a whole pile like that. When they came back within an hour or so, I had all that done. They were surprised. They thought, "Oh my god, this guy just cut anywhere!" He took his ruler out. He measured ten of them right in a row. Right on. So he says, "Hey, where'd you learn all this?" I said, "Common sense." I said, "I put a c-clamp on there on that stop so it wouldn't move. So it can't move one way or another. And then I could just put the thing in there, boom, boom, boom, boom, throw everything on my lap to cut them this long. He said, "Hey, anymore guys like you?" I said, "Yeah, I got some friends that want job." He said, "Yeah, I could get some more contracts." He said, "Tell them, 'Come in!'" So about two days later, I got them in and they work. And get many more.
All of a sudden, working the punch press, spot welding, lot of spot welding, my appendix went out. After about two weeks or three weeks of work over there, appendix went out. I didn't have no money. It cost $200 for hospital appendix. So I told the boss, "Hey, looks like I got to go back to camp again." "Why?" I said, "Because I haven't got the money to pay." He said, "Don't worry about it. I'll pay it. You pay me back any amount you want every week." So that helped me. So I did that. I was laid up about ten, twelve days for appendix. Then I went back to work again. And I couldn't do heavy work.
We had about ten people hired by then before I left. So he said, "I'll tell you what, you're going to be supervisor over here." And I got a job as a supervisor. Then we got more guys. I had at least hundred people working. Then I got my brothers in there and my brother-in-law and a lot of my friends.
REDMAN: So this crew that you're supervising, are they almost -- ?
TOMINAGA: Yeah, yeah, defense. All kinds of things we made. But one thing is, we made radio guards. It was very important. And the {last?} bar for the Army {stove?} That was the two biggest things that we made for the Army. He was really making the money, too. He could only make 10 percent above cost. The defense plant couldn't make that kind of money. They were tied down. But he still made money.
REDMAN: The crew that you were supervising, were they almost all Japanese men?
TOMINAGA: All Japanese. There was one Italian fellow that was working as the welder there before, one Polish guy that used to be the grinder, and one girl. I think she was part German from out that way. That's all they had there. And we went in, the Japanese. And I had all Japanese working.
REDMAN: And these people were happy to have a job?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. They were happy to have a job. And they started out with seventy-five cents an hour. Then after so much, we gave them a little raise. I was only making ninety-five cents an hour.
REDMAN: What was the living situation like? Where did people live when they were working there?
TOMINAGA: Oh, we all lived in different apartments.
REDMAN: So there was less restriction, certainly, in Chicago than in San Francisco.
TOMINAGA: We could live anywhere we wanted, right. There was only certain places you couldn't go into. Naval depot or something like that. We couldn't go in. That was off limits.
REDMAN: Back at camp, I wonder if you could talk for just a moment about the medical situation there. Let's say you had gotten your appendectomy or appendicitis in camp rather than in Chicago. What would happen there?
TOMINAGA: They had a good hospital. They had real good nurses. In fact, one of my friend, he graduated University of California med school, just got out, and he was voted one of the top doctors at med school before he went into camp.
REDMAN: So people at camp were fortunate to have --
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. And then there was some others that was in business before that. You had real good doctors.
REDMAN: But one of the things that I've heard is maybe that those hospitals might not have had the same equipment as a city hospital.
TOMINAGA: Right. They couldn't have a lot of things the other hospital have. Because wartime on top of that. So they had to just do and make -- some of those doctors knew how to make certain things, make their own things.
REDMAN: How about funerals at camp?
TOMINAGA: They had funerals. They had different religions. Catholic and all. They had the ministers there.
REDMAN: So that was a regular occurrence, a part of life.
TOMINAGA: Right. Just like everyday life. Only thing, it was in a barracks instead of a church or temple.
REDMAN: Were you in Chicago at the end of the war?
TOMINAGA: Yes. I was in -- no. I'm going to tell you that. That's another thing. It was July. They finally called me. I had a deferment because I was supervisor.
