And finally, they called me, the draft board called me. I went to the draft board in Chicago and they said, "My God, what are you doing out here?" I was twenty-something years old, and I said, "Well, I had a good job." They said, "Oh, yeah, yeah." And I don't know how they ever picked on me. I kind of have a hunch that this girl, she was always jealous of me my job. And I think she wrote to the draft board. That's the only way, that's the reason I got taken. Anyway, I had my son already, 1944. They wanted to get me in the Army. So I was ready to go. But I says, "But I want to go in." See, we could come back to California then, in '45. So I said, "I want to go back to California. I got two homes in my name, and I want to open it up, one for my mother, my son, and my wife, and the other one my sister's house." I had that one so they could come and live in it.
So the draft board gave me one week. I was supposed to go on August 13, 1945, but they gave me one week. And during that one week, V-J Day came, August 15. And Roosevelt said, "No fathers, nobody over twenty-six." So I couldn't go in. I wanted to go in; I was all ready. Everything packed, all ready to go in. I really wanted to go. My wife didn't want me to go. In fact, I was going to volunteer in camp. And you know what she said? "You volunteer and you probably won't see me when you come back." In other words, take a choice. Me or her. So I didn't volunteer. Otherwise, I would have gone too, with my brother.
REDMAN: Let's talk for a moment about Hiroshima and Nagasaki, when the bombs are dropped, the atomic bombs. What was your reaction to hearing that news?
TOMINAGA: Well, the atomic bomb didn't really hit me that hard. I didn't know it was that bad until much later on. You know, I just thought it was just another bomb. Most of us didn't know what the atomic bomb could do. We just thought it was another bomb, right?
REDMAN: How about V-J Day?
TOMINAGA: V-J Day. Where was I at? I guess I must have been -- I can't even remember what V-J Day was. Anyway, they was throwing stuff out of the window and all. I forget where I was at. I think I was here in Berkeley.
REDMAN: That was a pretty eventful week in your life, from the moment when you had that meeting with the draft board to getting out here was essentially the same moment as the end of the war. The war is ending right in that August of 1945.
TOMINAGA: Everything was happening that week. It was a big week.
REDMAN: So that's why some of the memories are harder there, but I wonder if you could tell me what your mood was at the end of the war. Do you recall feeling very happy or relieve d ? O r w h a t w a s y o u r f e e l i n g ?
T O M I N A G A : W e l l , I w a s n a t u r a l l y h a p p y t h e w a r e n d e d . A n d I w a s j u s t w o n d e r i n g w h a t s g o i n g t o h a p p e n . T h e n I h e a r d A d m i r a l N i m i t z w a s g o i n g t o r i d e e m p e r o r s w h i t e h o r s e [ H i r o h i t o / S h M w a ] , w h i c h h e d i d n t d o [ N o t e : I t w a s a c t u a l l y A d m i r a l W i l l i a m H a l s e y , J r . w h o s a i d t h i s , a n d N i m i t z o b j e c t e d ] . H e h a d t h a t r e s p e c t . H e d i d n t g o - - N i m i t z w a s a g o o d m a n .
I h a p p e n e d t o k n o w h i m p e r s o n a l l y a f t e r t h e w a r b e c a u s e h e l i v e d u p i n B e r k e l e y H i l l s . Y e a h . A n d t h e n h e w e n t i n t o T r e a s u r e Island. So a lot of people wonder how I met Admiral Nimitz. He was Nature Boy. He used to walk all around with his cane in his shorts. He always, "Hi, Joe! Hi, Joe!" I used to work next door to him. See, I was gardening. This one home, his backyard and this home had a horseshoe pit where I worked. And he used to like to pitch horseshoe and he used to pitch horseshoe and used to sleep out there naked on the table where no one could see him but me.
REDMAN: So that's amazing that you came to know this man who you were probably reading about in the papers.
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah, yeah. I read about Nimitz all the time. You see in the paper.
REDMAN: He probably could have had a pretty successful political career if he wanted.
TOMINAGA: Yeah. He was a nice man.
REDMAN: The last question I want to have on this tape -- and I'm going to start a third and final tape just to wrap up -- but the last question that I have for this is, do you have anything else to say about the end of the war, those few months and some of the decisions that you were making at the end of the war? You knew you wanted to be in Berkeley. Your wife was happy with that decision, to be in California?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. In fact, I wanted to come back, and then I came back to Berkeley. And then I didn't like work over here at all. I liked Chicago. So I packed everything and I went back to Chicago to live again. Naturally, my job over at this Wire Craft was taken over by some other fellow. They weren't doing that good, either. They put me on a good machine and all, but I wasn't happy there. My brother was doing body fender work. He said, "Joe, what are you good at?" Every Saturday I would go learn about the fender. And I learned about the fender. Had a good German boss. In fact, he even looked like Hitler. And he really treated me nice. Every Saturday I worked for about three months. And you know what he told me one day? He said, "You know what?" I thought he was going to fire me. But he said, "No! I got good news for you! You could become a union man now." So I got in the union, apprentice first. And they said, "You could be a journeyman." I got to be a Journeyman right away. And he really loved my work. I worked right near my home, too. In fact, across the street was the Diamond K. Tokyo Rose's father and brother owned the store. We looked out the third-floor and we looked right into the store. I saw when Tokyo Rose was getting through there, and a lot of people didn't know who she was.
REDMAN: So how long did you stay in Chicago before coming back to California?
TOMINAGA: About eight years. 1950, January 13. My wife didn't want to come back to California. Her mother and her brother was living in an apartment over there. We were all living together. So I left her there. We were kind of getting away from each other, too. So I brought my son and I; we drove all the way back in January through all the snow and everything. And I had my son, we were living here about six months before my wife decided, no, she better come and live with us. That's why I accept her back. We were separated for a short time. And a lot of people don't know that.
REDMAN: And then you reconcile back in the Bay Area.
TOMINAGA: Yeah, right. And then we lived together for fifty-five years before she passed on.
REDMAN: How many children do you end up having?
TOMINAGA: Only one and only.
REDMAN: My name is Sam Redman. Today is November 26. I'm in Albany, California. This is my third tape with Joe Tominaga. We left off talking about the end of the war. I just have a few questions about what happens, then, years later. We talked about the racism and the prejudice and some of the challenges the Japanese faced in the 1930s. And then some of that becomes very heated as the war arrives and there's more racism, more prejudice. What are the major problems for Japanese at the end of the war, as they face looking into this post-war world, was there still some racism, do you feel like, against the Japanese? Or had that sort of faded away?
TOMINAGA: Well, I never had it. Some people had it bad. They went out of the way. That's why they got treated that way. I went along with whatever we supposed to go. They said, "Go down that line," that's where I went. That's why I never had much problem like some people did. Some people got called this and that and everything. Well, I never was called that.
Only one thing, though, during the time in Chicago just before I came back. The reason I quit Chicago. There was one man in there that called me a "God damn Jap." He said, "No wonder there's Pearl Harbor." Oh, I didn't like that. So I went right downstairs right away and I told the boss, "I quit!" "Why you quit?" I told him why I quit. I said, "Because of him." My regular boss upstairs, too, he couldn't understand it. He was surprised I would have such a short temper that I quit. I quit. I was good with body fenders. I was making money for him, too.
REDMAN: So that prompts your coming back to California.
TOMINAGA: Yeah, yeah. That made me come back to California. And I wanted my son to get good education.
REDMAN: And you felt like he could get a better education, have better opportunities --
TOMINAGA: Than in Chicago. Those Chicago schools weren't that great.
REDMAN: So years later, flashing forward then to the early 1980s, there starts to be a movement amongst both Japanese-American political leaders, but then also cultural leaders, and community organizations to make some sort of both an official apology for what happens to Japanese Americans during the war, but then also to make a payment of some lost wages, of some lost --
TOMINAGA: Well, when they said, "We're going to get money," I said, "Take it or leave it." If they give it to me -- my son, he was born '44, before '45, so he have a chance to get it, too. He said, "I don't want it, Dad." He said, "I don't want it. I don't deserve it." He was in Cal already, see, and he tells me, "That will hurt me." I said, "No. You could have been born in California and all if it wasn't for the war." I said, "You should." He borrowed money to go to school, too. So, you know, that helped him to pay off his debt.
REDMAN: Was that an important symbol for you personally, to hear a US President years later, many years later, make that sort of apology and write those -- ?
TOMINAGA: Well, it took a lot of talking and a lot of doing to do it.
REDMAN: Whereas you may not agree with the Japanese-American community all the time, and the JACL all the time, it seems like you would recognize the importance of that activism in getting that to come to bear. You mentioned that there was a lot of talking and organizing it took to make that happen.
TOMINAGA: Yeah. So I said, "If they give it to me, I'll take it. If they don't give it to me, that's all right." I said a lot of people lost a lot of money, but, I said, our family, we didn't lose that much. We didn't even lose one chopstick. And so, I didn't have against them because we had our thing in the right place. I had a good real estate man over here. I had a lot of good Caucasian friends, too, here that took care of my cars, two cars that I owned. Lot of people didn't all those good friends. Depending on each family, they had good friends. If you had neighbors that was against you and all, they went out of the way to break into your house and all that. It was just depending on how you act and how you got treated.
REDMAN: Do you have any other thoughts on that movement in the 1980s? Do you think that that was good for the Japanese-American community, to recognize what had happened rather than -- ?
TOMINAGA: Oh, yeah. I always knew that was wrong for them to make me leave, being an American citizen. The Germans, the Italians, they weren't treated the way we were treated. So that really got me. If they were all treated that way, too, I don't think I would talk so bad. Deep, deep down in my heart -- I didn't go out of the way -- but I thought that was wrong.
That's why I was against -- politically, I was against Democrats. Democrat president was the one that put me in there. And [Earl] Warren, he was a Democrat, a governor, and [General John L.] DeWitt, he was a Democrat. {Tolan?}, he was a Democrat. All those people that was really against me. That's why I always was a Republican. That's my way of telling people why I'm not a Democrat.
REDMAN: This is my last question. It's always the hardest. If you look back now, today, many years later, on World War II, in the place of your life, and how that shaped you as a man, what do you think back on the war years?
TOMINAGA: No, I thought that I gained in a way. I got to see whole different part of the country, meet different people. You go to Chicago, you meet all Polish people, one group, Italian, another group, German; you'd be surprised that they all lived in a different area. Each one. I think I gained and I think a lot of people gained from it. There was lot of my friends, who live and never got to go to college, were smart, too. But, because they had to stay on the farm to help the father and mother, two acre farm pulling beets and carrots and take them to the city, and they had no chance to go to college. But those boys, they were smart, too, in school. So they really gained from it.
One lady, a Caucasian lady in Fresno area -- I heard this from another friend -- said, "I'm glad the Japs gone. Now my daughter has a chance to be valedictorian at school." Because every school has Japanese who are really always on top. Now, you don't see them at all. Mostly, Vietnamese and Chinese are always up top. Japanese not in there. They're just not the way they used to be.
REDMAN: Do you have any final thoughts that you'd like to add? We've talked about a lot of topics today. And I so appreciate all the time you've given me. We've talked about your parents, how they arrived in the United States, the arrival of war, all of the things that happen in your life after the war, from the Assembly Center to Topaz to going to Chicago. Is there anything else you'd like to add?
TOMINAGA: One thing I'd like to really add is, in a way, I'm glad the war came on. That's why the Oriental, Chinese even, could not buy land, right? After the war, they got to buy land and get naturalized. My mother got naturalized. She could just barely write her name. But she went through all this and that, and she didn't know what was what. But she just went. They said, "Go to San Francisco. Go there, and see what the judge says." And the judge asked me who I was and all that, asked questions, and I told him "My mother had four sons in the Army, and she can't read or write Japanese." And he said, "Why, you have four sons in the service?" He says, "Tell her raise her right hand." So I tell her raise her right hand. She got naturalized that way. If it wasn't for me to talk to the judge, she would never got naturalized. And a lot of people thought that was unfair.
REDMAN: But in your mind, the war -- before the war, we talked about the Asian Exclusion Act, we talked about all of those things, we talked about the Japanese not being able to own land. After the war, a lot of that just has to go away.
TOMINAGA: Yeah. And I think that we gained. The Japanese people, the Chinese, too. We really gained with the war. Japanese and Chinese never got along before. The Chinese would not let their children talk to Japanese or do anything. In fact, Chinese didn't let their kids get out of Chinatown. Lot of people don't know that. They had to stay in Chinatown to get married and all.
REDMAN: So that opens up some of those constraints that had been there before, in some sense. Well, é ó ô E F O P ^ _ f g z " --  --  ² ³ ¼ ½ Î Ï Ö ×
